statement: BlueprintProvider derives a normalized BlueprintSnapshot and BlueprintDelta for a requested commit and reports capability flags; GRAPHREFLY_STACK_BLUEPRINT_PROVIDER selects the implementation, and only graphrefly is supported in the MVP.

Each indexed commit in the flagship stack resolves to a deterministic BlueprintSnapshot hash; selecting a commit compares its snapshot with its semantic stack parent and distinguishes structural delta from affected-but-structurally-unchanged claim impact. Merge-parent selection is deferred outside the linear flagship stack.

A SemanticChangeRecord binds a commit or work unit to intent, blueprint claims, allowed source scopes, dependencies, required checks, and freshness witnesses. GPT-5.6 or a human may propose it, but it is not evidence until deterministic validation accepts and binds it.

Evidence is read-only reproducible input or output such as hashes, deltas, validated claims, approvals, and check results. GateResult is the read-only output of a deterministic function over current repository state, witnesses, contracts, policy, and evidence; local CLI and future CI runners execute the same function and cannot hand-edit its verdict.

The primary UI is a real Git commit and branch graph. Commit selection synchronizes a focused Blueprint structural delta, claim-impact context, Semantic Change Card, GateResult, reason codes, checks, reviewer actions, and an optional raw-diff drilldown.

The live path uses Codex with GRAPHREFLY_STACK_MODEL and GRAPHREFLY_STACK_REASONING_EFFORT overrides, defaults to gpt-5.6-sol and high, and records the exact effective model, effort, prompt version, and provider response provenance without allowing model output to set GateResult.

The v1 wire family is strict JSON Schema draft-07 under contracts/v1/schemas with URN schema IDs, required properties, additionalProperties false, no coercion or defaults, and pinned Ajv 8.20.0 validation for Task, GitStack, BlueprintProviderCapabilities, BlueprintSnapshot, BlueprintDelta, ChangePlan and WorkUnit, SemanticChangeRecord, CheckResult, GateResult, SelectiveReplan, BundleManifest, ReviewDecision, and golden cases.

GraphReFly topology hashes use upstream canonicalTopologyBytes with SHA-256. Stack artifact hashes use RFC 8785 JCS, UTF-8, and SHA-256; inputs must satisfy I-JSON, manifest and README are excluded from self-referential artifact hashes, and gate input digests exclude GateResult, model explanation, ReviewDecision, wall-clock metadata, and randomness.

A stable Git patch ID is only a rebase candidate key. Acceptance proves the current Git object, whitespace-sensitive canonical diff digest, actual changed paths, semantic parent, and required checks. Records are immutable; a successful unaffected rebase creates a new deterministic SemanticChangeRecord with rebindFrom rather than mutating or blindly trusting the prior record.

Each accepted SemanticChangeRecord binds commit OID, stable patch ID, canonical diff digest, semantic parent record and commit, blueprint hash, policy revision, allowed source-scope digest, required-check IDs, contract version, and provider ID and version. A blueprint hash change triggers claim-impact revalidation; it invalidates only impacted claims and may deterministically rebind unaffected work.

Gate validation order is schema, provider capability, Git binding, source scope, semantic parent and dependencies, blueprint and policy freshness, required checks, then artifact integrity. Verdict is pass, blocked, or error; work units follow ChangePlan order and reasons follow validation stage then code order. The v1 reason-code set is schema invalid, provider capability unsupported, blueprint diagnostics error, commit not found, commit binding mismatch, patch ID ambiguous, source scope violation, semantic parent stale, dependency invalid, blueprint witness stale, policy revision stale, session write requires broker, required check missing, required check failed, and artifact hash mismatch.

The GraphReFly v1 provider reports commit snapshots, canonical topology bytes, node metadata, subgraphs, diagnostics, and structural delta as supported; merge-parent selection is unsupported, and claim-impact evaluation belongs to Stack core rather than the provider.

ReviewDecision is separately attributed human evidence and never edits or supplies GateResult. The semantic v1 artifact remains approve, defer, or reject evidence bound to SemanticChangeRecord IDs; a generic repository review uses its own strict decision artifact bound to the exact parent commit, selected commit, Blueprint hash, base and head range. The authless local product labels a reviewer without claiming verified identity, and the UI presents deterministic facts, any GateResult, and the human decision as distinct states.

STACK-3 locks byte-stable current-valid, clean-rebase-semantic-stale, unrelated-change-still-valid, stale-parent, forged-or-wrong-scope, and fresh-selective-replan cases. Every case validates against v1 schemas, canonicalizes repeatably, matches a checked-in SHA-256 digest, and fixes GateResult verdict and reason ordering without implementing the STACK-5 gate engine.

The private pnpm workspace has four TypeScript boundaries: @graphrefly-stack/contracts owns strict v1 validation, JCS and artifact digests; @graphrefly-stack/core owns deterministic gate, freshness, impact and replan behavior plus ports; @graphrefly-stack/cli is the only composition root and owns the executable, system adapters and local HTTP server; @graphrefly-stack/review is a React 19 and Vite 8 projection that consumes versioned data and never owns verdict semantics. Production review runs in the CLI process without a database or services split.

The canonical graphrefly-stack surface exposes fixture create, plan, gate, replan, review and export. Every non-review command supports --json and emits exactly one versioned envelope to stdout while diagnostics use stderr; execution success exits 0, a successfully computed blocked GateResult exits 2, and usage, schema or runtime failure exits 1. The review command binds to 127.0.0.1 by default and serves the built web projection from the same local process.

The CLI composition root invokes system Git with shell disabled and allowlisted argv, confines fixture mutations to an explicit output repository, and derives GraphReFly snapshots for the flagship through a fixed entrypoint in a detached worktree using a scrubbed environment, read-only Node permission allowlists and a timeout. The core defines GitAdapter, BlueprintProvider and PlanProvider ports; only system Git, graphrefly and replay or server-side Codex plan providers are implemented in the MVP, and browser code never receives credentials.

Source templates and reviewed redacted replay inputs live under fixtures; generated Git repositories, detached worktrees and raw provider responses live only under ignored .private paths; only redacted hash-verified bundles enter evidence/runs. Replay is the default plan mode and requires no credentials. Live must be selected explicitly, validates against the same contracts, records exact provenance, and cannot silently become replay; an explicitly enabled provider-failure fallback is labelled replay-fallback with the failure reason and replay artifact identity.

The server-side Codex TypeScript SDK runs plan and selective-replan turns read-only with approval never, network tools disabled, an explicit model and reasoning effort, and per-turn structured output schemas. Stack post-validates the strict proposal contract, preserves locked work-unit IDs, dependencies, scopes, claim IDs, checks and selective invalidation boundaries, then deterministically binds proposals without allowing model output to create or edit GateResult. Raw responses remain under .private; redacted live-run artifacts record SDK and CLI runtime versions, prompt version, thread, usage, response and output digests, and enter an explicitly selected bundle. Provider failure exits as live error unless replay fallback was explicitly enabled and labelled.

The flagship GraphReFly entrypoint imports the pinned upstream runtime, builds the repository's actual graph modules, and returns graph.blueprint({diagnostics:true}) output with caller provenance and upstream hashing plus describeToMermaid(graph.describe()) readable diagram source; Stack must reject provider payloads that are not GraphBlueprint v1, whose topology hash does not match upstream canonicalTopologyBytes, or whose diagram source is not the GraphReFly render projection of the same runtime graph. Review data includes A1 and per-work-unit commit snapshots plus deterministic parent deltas derived from those snapshots. Commit selection renders the GraphReFly-generated diagram with added and removed structure distinguished, and parses the canonical Git patch into file, hunk, old-line, and new-line rows for a GitHub-style viewer without treating raw patch text as the user interface.

The first production stack contract orders WorkUnits linearly for discovery, evaluation, and review, but a WorkUnit ID is a stable logical identity rather than an index or commit OID. Each accepted SemanticChangeRecord binds that identity to the current immutable Git object and explicit semantic parent; rebases create new bindings, and no v1 field may require encoding a WorkUnit's linear position into its identity.

A hosted control-plane envelope contains only schema-validated redacted evidence explicitly selected by repository policy, with content digests, redaction policy revision, runner provenance, and artifact allowlists. Raw source, unredacted GraphBlueprints, raw model or provider output, credentials, and repository execution remain local by default; broader uploads require an explicit scoped authorization recorded as evidence.

Before production repository generalization, a pinned published @graphrefly/ts release based on 0.2.1 or later must expose version-aware GraphBlueprint parsing and validation, verification of canonical topology hashes, deterministic readable rendering whose input is the GraphBlueprint itself, and canonical structural delta semantics including nodes, edges, metadata, and subgraphs. Stack validates the advertised upstream capabilities and retains ownership only of commit snapshots, Git bindings, claim impact, policy, evidence, and GateResult.

A repository is supported by the first usable product when it is a local Git worktree, pins a supported @graphrefly/ts release, exposes a repository-configured executable Blueprint entrypoint that returns the upstream GraphBlueprint contract without credentials or writes, and supplies resolvable linear base and head revisions. Stack resolves the target repository's pinned runtime, executes each revision in an isolated detached worktree, and fails closed on missing entrypoints, unsupported Blueprint capabilities, diagnostics errors, non-linear ancestry, or hash and renderer verification failure.

For a compatible repository and linear base-to-head range, Stack derives the ordered commits from Git rather than a ChangePlan fixture, assigns display identities independently of U1/U2/U3, snapshots each commit with the target repository's GraphReFly runtime, compares each snapshot to its first-parent stack predecessor using upstream structural delta semantics, parses each canonical Git patch into file, hunk, old-line, and new-line rows, and serves one synchronized review payload directly from those facts. Claims and GateResult may be absent when the repository has not adopted semantic records; their absence may not block structural stack review or be presented as a passing semantic gate.

The generic repository runner accepts only the pinned published upstream capability set whose graph.blueprint() emits GraphBlueprint v2 with non-empty sibling-unique Graph-owned mountId values on every non-root subgraph. It parses and freezes artifacts with parseGraphBlueprint, verifies recorded topology hashes with verifyBlueprintHash and caller-owned hashing, produces readable diagram source only through blueprintToMermaid, and computes each equal-version parent delta only through diffGraphBlueprints. Unsupported versions, missing or duplicate mount identity, diagnostics errors, version transitions, malformed evidence, hash mismatch, and helper failure produce an explicit unavailable/error state rather than a Stack-derived fallback diagram or delta.

The strict graphrefly.stack.repository.v1 config at .graphrefly-stack.json contains only schema and blueprint.entrypoint, with a normalized repository-relative file path that may not escape the worktree. The canonical command is graphrefly-stack review --repo <path> --base <revision> --head <revision>. Its graphrefly.stack.review.v1 payload records repository label, exact target GraphReFly version, configured entrypoint, resolved base and head OIDs, and ordered ReviewCommit values containing immutable OID, parent OID, subject, hashed GraphBlueprint v2, upstream Mermaid source, upstream parent delta events, and a server-parsed structured Git diff. The base Blueprint is evidence for the first delta but is not a synthetic WorkUnit. Semantic status is explicitly not-configured when accepted records are absent, and no validity verdict is emitted. Entrypoint stdout must be exactly one JSON value within the timeout and size bound; stderr, absolute paths, environment values, and raw provider output do not enter the browser payload.

A checked-in historical run consists only of a small human index and one strict portable evidence bundle that embeds one BundleManifest and exactly the manifest-bound logical artifacts. The loader accepts the portable file directly or its containing directory, rejects schema errors, duplicate or mismatched manifest paths, unmanifested embedded values, missing values, and canonical SHA-256 mismatches, then projects review data from verified in-memory artifacts. Expanded artifact paths are an internal logical namespace, not checked-in files; ordinary exports, generated repositories, worktrees, raw provider output, local review records, comments and caches remain outside the working tree under ignored private paths or .git/grfs. Only an explicit content-addressed export may move policy-allowed comments, decisions or redacted evidence into a portable bundle for sharing or future hosted upload.

The public package is @graphrefly/stack and exposes only the grfs binary. Its packed files contain the executable, local review UI, JSON schemas, and required replay assets without monorepo-relative runtime reads or workspace dependencies. The reproducible MVP path remains a repository-local developer dependency invoked with pnpm exec grfs; a future global launcher may be optional but must prefer the repository-local engine rather than silently replacing its version. grfs init --repo <path> --graph-module <path> [--graph-export <name>] creates the strict config and a deterministic root adapter, refusing path escape or overwrite unless --force. Review snapshots the configured bounded adapter bytes and injects them only into isolated detached revisions where the entrypoint is absent, so onboarding after an existing stack does not require rewriting Git history; the referenced root Graph module remains revision-owned and must exist. grfs review resolves one installed exact @graphrefly/ts version in >=0.3.0 <0.4.0, validates required public Blueprint v2 capabilities, and requires every reviewed revision's dependencies or devDependencies declaration to accept that exact version. Recognized lockfiles must exist but may change content or type across the range. npm-pack acceptance installs the tarball into an independent repository, runs init and review through pnpm exec grfs across registry-style symlink execution and pre-onboarding commits, serves the embedded UI, and proves no workspace path is required.

The installed @graphrefly/stack package exposes the grfs binary with init, fixture create, plan, gate, replan, review, and export commands. Every command supports a versioned --json envelope on stdout while diagnostics use stderr; success exits 0, a successfully computed blocked GateResult exits 2, and usage, schema, or runtime failure exits 1. Non-JSON review binds only to 127.0.0.1 and serves the embedded read-only web projection from the same local process.

The installed @graphrefly/stack package preserves the C30 command, JSON-envelope and exit-code protocol. Non-JSON review binds only to 127.0.0.1 and serves the embedded web projection from the same local process. Generic repository review remains read-only for source files, Git objects, refs, GateResult and repository configuration, but may expose one bounded same-origin application/json endpoint that validates and appends strict immutable local review decisions below the real Git common directory at .git/grfs. Each decision binds base, head, parent and selected commit OIDs plus Blueprint hash, records an unverified local reviewer, summary and timestamp, and is never rebound after Git or Blueprint changes. GET projection and explicit download may read only records matching the current reviewed witnesses. A portable review export contains only manifest-bound, content-hashed human review artifacts and their non-source witness identifiers; comments and decisions are eligible for later hosted upload only after explicit export and repository-policy redaction, while caches, raw source, unredacted Blueprints and execution details are never included by default.

Semantic adoption is opt-in and preserves the strict repository review config. A separately versioned repository policy and accepted ChangePlan are explicit Git-owned intent artifacts created only by an explicit CLI acceptance action before implementation; browser review never writes them. Each implementation commit contains exactly one GraphReFly-Work-Unit trailer pointing to a stable plan-owned WorkUnit identity. The trailer is a lookup key rather than evidence, does not encode linear position, and cannot replace validation of the commit, diff, semantic parent, source scope, Blueprint, policy or checks. Rebuildable drafts, derived SemanticChangeRecords, caches and check output live below the Git common directory at .git/grfs and may leave it only through a strict content-addressed portable export.

A production ClaimDraft uses a finite versioned predicate family over GraphReFly-owned Blueprint v2 facts such as stable node identity, edge relation, subgraph mount identity and allowlisted metadata, plus repository-owned source scopes, dependency identities, policy capabilities and required-check IDs. Model and human prose remain explanation only. Deterministic admission rejects unknown or ambiguous selectors, unsupported predicates, arbitrary commands, widened scopes, missing dependencies, unrecognized checks and policy references. Required checks resolve only to shell-disabled argv and execution bounds declared by the accepted repository policy. GraphReFly owns intrinsic Blueprint facts and Stack owns predicate admission, claim impact, policy freshness and gate semantics.

For an adopted linear repository, the canonical runner derives ordered commits from Git, resolves exactly one accepted WorkUnit binding per implementation commit, executes each revision through the existing permission-limited GraphReFly provider, deterministically constructs immutable SemanticChangeRecords and evaluates schema, provider, Git binding, source scope, semantic parent and dependencies, Blueprint and policy freshness, required checks and artifact integrity in fixed order. Invalid configuration, artifacts, runtime or ambiguous bindings are errors; reproducibly unmet scope, freshness, dependency, policy or checks are blocked; pass requires every unit to be valid. A Blueprint or policy change reevaluates only referenced claims, may immutably rebind unaffected work, and permits a selective replan to replace only proven-invalid WorkUnits. Repositories without accepted semantic artifacts remain structural not-configured reviews and never receive a passing GateResult.

Replay remains the no-credential default. Live semantic plan or replan requires an explicit content manifest identifying the task, Blueprint fields, repository-policy facts and source paths authorized for model transmission; the CLI presents and hashes that manifest, and no unlisted source, full Blueprint, credentials, environment values or local execution details may enter the request. The Codex provider remains read-only and proposal-only, records exact model, effort, prompt, manifest and output provenance, keeps raw responses below ignored private storage, and fails rather than silently widening context or impersonating replay.

The generic review UI adds semantic plan intent, typed claim impact, deterministic GateResult, reason codes, checks and selective recovery only when accepted semantic artifacts exist. Commit, Blueprint, parent delta and code diff remain synchronized; decision, model proposal and GateResult remain visibly distinct. The first view answers pass, blocked or error and which WorkUnits need action, exact witnesses appear next, and source, runtime and provenance stay on demand. The UI may write bounded local ReviewDecision records but cannot accept repository plans, edit policy, alter bindings, run arbitrary checks, mutate Git or change GateResult.

Semantic lifecycle v1 uses urn:graphrefly-stack:schema:semantic-artifacts:v1 and urn:graphrefly-stack:schema:semantic-golden-suite:v1 as a strict additive contract family separate from graphrefly.stack.repository.v1. Repository policy, accepted plan, commit binding, model context, check result, semantic record, gate input, gate result and selective replan use explicit schema tags; repository paths are normalized relative forward-slash paths; subgraph mount paths are ordered identities; stable bytes use RFC 8785 JSON Canonicalization Scheme and SHA-256. Gate reasons use the fixed exported v1 order from schema failure through artifact hash mismatch. The twelve checked-in golden cases and their per-case digests are packaged with both schemas, while adoption storage remains .graphrefly-stack/policy.json, .graphrefly-stack/plans and Git-common-directory .git/grfs without extending or weakening the existing strict repository config.

Generic grfs plan derives its base commit and verified GraphReFly Blueprint v2 hash from the target repository. Replay admits one strict human SemanticPlanProposal; live mode requires both a strict ModelContextManifest and an explicit --authorize-context flag, projects only manifest-listed policy identities, source roots, capabilities and check IDs plus listed Blueprint fields and bounded source paths, gives Codex a minimal anyOf structured-output schema, and then revalidates the output against the full canonical contract. Drafts and raw provenance are content-addressed below .git/grfs. Only --accept with a local reviewer label writes the exact policy and AcceptedChangePlan under .graphrefly-stack, without staging or committing them. The next Git commit is a plan-acceptance commit containing only those intent artifacts. Generic --bind reads accepted artifacts and implementation history only through Git objects, requires a merge-free linear range and exactly one known GraphReFly-Work-Unit trailer per implementation commit in dependency order, and derives schema-valid immutable bindings into content-addressed .git/grfs state without reading or changing source workspace files, refs or objects.

Generic semantic execution reads accepted policy, plan and optional selective-replan artifacts from Git, reconstructs linear bindings, derives each WorkUnit Blueprint through the permission-limited provider, evaluates only the finite v1 predicate family, runs only accepted shell-disabled argv in a detached worktree with timeout and fail-closed network isolation, and emits strict immutable records plus GateInput and GateResult in canonical reason order. Prior local records are accepted only from hash-addressed schema-valid gate bundles; an unchanged stable patch and exact claim witness may rebind immutably, while changed or unsatisfied witnesses invalidate only impacted units and declared dependants. Replay or explicitly authorized live replan must replace exactly the GateResult-invalid identities, writes a Git-owned SelectiveReplan beside a new accepted plan only on explicit acceptance, reuses byte-identical preserved WorkUnits and their original bindings, and requires new implementation commits only for invalid units. Semantic review is an additive read-only projection separate from ReviewDecision, and the strict portable export contains hashes, policy, plan, bindings, records, checks and GateResult but excludes source, raw Blueprint, raw check output, environment, credentials and model response.

CI integration is an additive strict contract family with graphrefly.stack.ci-invocation.v1 and graphrefly.stack.ci-result.v1 artifacts. The invocation binds adapter and contract version, provider, repository and owner immutable IDs, event name, base and head Git OIDs, pull-request number, workflow ref and SHA, run ID and attempt, actor ID, unique job name, selected plan ID, repository policy digest, exact @graphrefly/stack and @graphrefly/ts versions and cache-key inputs. The result embeds or hash-binds the unmodified semantic GateResult, its GateInput digest, adapter outcome, run provenance, redaction statement and portable bundle digest; it labels GitHub event identity as platform-asserted inside the job but not independently verified outside GitHub. RFC 8785 JCS and SHA-256 govern stable bytes, wall-clock and cancellation metadata do not enter GateInput, and adapters may not add, remove, reorder or reinterpret gate reasons.

The first CI adapter exposes explicit grfs ci init and grfs ci run surfaces. Init writes one deterministic .github/workflows/graphrefly-stack.yml only by explicit request and refuses overwrite unless forced. The workflow triggers only pull_request, uses the immutable pull-request head rather than the synthetic merge ref, fetches sufficient Git history, requires the current event base to be an ancestor of head, and invokes the repository-local pnpm exec grfs after a frozen install with lifecycle scripts disabled. The job has a globally distinctive GraphReFly Stack semantic-gate name, permissions contents read and every write, secrets and id-token capability disabled; fork and same-repository pull requests follow the same least-privilege path. Pass exits success; blocked and error both fail the job while retaining their distinct GateResult. A content-digest-named redacted artifact is retained seven days, the job summary shows only verdict, affected WorkUnits and reason codes, and no source, raw Blueprint, raw output, environment or credential is uploaded. Package-manager caching keys operating system, Node, pnpm, lockfile, Stack and GraphReFly versions; .git/grfs, worktrees, check output and gate artifacts are never restored from cache.

CI run validates the GitHub pull_request event schema, resolves the checked-out object to the event head SHA, rejects head or base drift, requires one merge-free ancestry path and never evaluates the pull-request merge ref. An explicit plan ID must resolve to one accepted plan whose binding consumes the current tip; without it, the adapter validates every reachable accepted plan and requires exactly one tip-covering candidate, otherwise CI_PLAN_NOT_FOUND or CI_PLAN_AMBIGUOUS is an adapter error. Concurrency is keyed by immutable repository ID and pull-request number with cancel-in-progress; every run and rerun recomputes GateInput from current Git objects and records run attempt, while a canceled or superseded run never yields a reusable success artifact. Malformed event, dependency or runtime mismatch, lifecycle-script requirement, missing deny-network sandbox, unsupported topology, stale head, plan ambiguity, artifact tamper and export verification failure are errors rather than blocked semantic verdicts. Direct push, pull_request_target and merge_group are unsupported, and merge-queue parity is owned by PRODUCT-DAG after merge-parent semantics are locked.

Hosted domain identities for tenant, repository, actor and membership are provider-neutral immutable IDs with a GitHub adapter that maps a durable GitHub App installation account, selected repository ID and provider user ID. Roles are owner, admin, reviewer and viewer. Effective repository access is the intersection of an active Stack membership, current selected-repository installation access and the authenticated provider user's current repository access, revalidated on privileged reads and writes and invalidated by installation, repository, membership, authorization, SSO or user-token revocation events. GitHub browser authentication uses state and PKCE with refreshable short-lived user credentials; automation uses installation credentials only for installation administration and never personal access tokens. Unknown, stale or unavailable authorization fails closed rather than using cached access to reveal evidence.

The strict additive hosted contract family defines graphrefly.stack.hosted-envelope.v1 and a repository-owned hosted-sync workflow separate from graphrefly-stack.yml. The sync job runs after the semantic gate, never checks out or executes repository or pull-request code, requests only actions read and id-token write, contains no repository write permission or secret, and presents a GitHub OIDC token whose issuer, audience, expiry, repository and owner IDs, workflow ref and SHA, run ID and attempt, actor ID, event and immutable revision exactly match the envelope and an allowed installation. The envelope selects exactly one fixed redaction profile: gate-summary-v1; explicit semantic-review-v1 containing the existing verified seven-artifact portable bundle; or explicit local-review-decisions-v1 containing exported non-source decisions. It binds schema and profile revisions, tenant and repository identities, CI invocation and GateInput digests, complete source-bundle digest, exact included artifact paths and hashes, fixed excluded sensitive classes and runner provenance. RFC 8785 JCS and SHA-256 govern stable bytes; the service validates every nested schema, membership and digest before tenant-and-repository-scoped idempotent storage, rejects replay under different claims, and never accepts raw source, unredacted Blueprint, raw model or check output, environment, credential or arbitrary extension fields.

One hosted control-plane API and web application owns PostgreSQL metadata for tenants, memberships, repositories, envelope indexes, decisions, audit and tombstones plus object storage for canonical envelope bytes. TLS protects transit and provider-managed production KMS keys protect storage; content-addressing and deduplication are scoped within one tenant and repository and never reveal cross-tenant digest existence. V1 operates in one disclosed United States region without BYOK. Full envelope content expires after 90 days; minimal GateResult indexes, hosted decisions, audit events and non-content deletion tombstones expire after 365 days. Deletion denies reads immediately, purges primary content within 24 hours and backup content within 30 days while audit retains no deleted payload. Canonical uncompressed envelope size is capped at 2 MiB, accepted uploads at 100 per repository per UTC day and retained content at 1 GiB per tenant; violations return explicit 413 or 429 outcomes, are audited without sensitive payload and cannot change GateResult.

A strict append-only graphrefly.stack.hosted-decision.v1 records approve, request-changes or defer from a currently authorized provider user, the immutable hosted actor identity, repository, envelope digest, exact GateResult and witness identities, bounded summary, server receipt time and audit event. It remains separate from GateResult and merge authority, cannot rewrite prior decisions and supersedes one only through an explicit new decision that references the predecessor. A local identityVerified false decision may be stored only with that status and never converted into authenticated hosted authorship. Every authentication, membership, repository link, upload accept or reject, evidence read, decision append, authorization denial and delete action emits an immutable tenant-scoped audit event with actor, action, target identity, outcome and timestamp but no source or sensitive artifact payload; owner and admin may export their tenant audit records.

The hosted web projection is read-only except for the C46 append-decision endpoint. Its first view shows immutable GateResult and upload status as separate states, then affected WorkUnits and ordered reasons, exact redacted witnesses, provenance, decision history and audit on demand, with links back to repository-owned source review rather than copied source. It cannot accept plans, mutate policy, Git, checks, repository or merge state, execute code, coordinate reservations or change the verdict. Hosted upload is not a required semantic-gate dependency: unavailable, timed-out or rejected sync remains pending or rejected without translating GateResult; the runner may retry the same digest idempotently from the seven-day CI artifact, and the UI states when evidence is expired, deleted, unauthorized or not yet synced instead of presenting a stale success.

The additive PR integration contract defines strict graphrefly.stack.integration-candidate.v1 and graphrefly.stack.integration-result.v1 artifacts. One candidate binds repository and provider-neutral identities; exact unique merge-base, current target and immutable head Git OIDs; merge algorithm revision; accepted plan and policy digests; verified base, target and head Blueprint hashes; both upstream GraphReFly delta digests; the isolated merged tree identity and verified candidate Blueprint hash when textual merge succeeds; and the unchanged head GateInput and GateResult identities. The repository-owned runner creates the candidate in temporary storage without changing source worktree files, refs or commits and uses the target repository's supported exact GraphReFly runtime to parse, hash and diff Blueprint v2 evidence. Result outcome is compatible, conflict or error with fixed ordered reasons and exact path, node, edge, subgraph, metadata-field, claim, dependency, policy or diagnostics witnesses. Shared identity alone is non-blocking; deterministic incompatible final effects, delete-versus-change, candidate diagnostics or execution failure, stale claim, dependency or policy witness, text conflict, ambiguous ancestry, identity drift and artifact tamper fail closed. Target or head movement invalidates the result. IntegrationResult remains separate from GateResult, ReviewDecision and merge authority; v1 requires one unique merge base and a merge-free contributor range and excludes merge commits, merge queues, arbitrary DAG review, reservations, actor edit credentials and repository mutation.

The additive graphrefly.stack.git-topology-slice.v2 contract binds repository and provider-neutral identities, one explicit ancestor base and one head, exact ordered Git objects and parent OIDs, at most 64 selected objects, maximum topological width eight and at most two parents per object. Every object binds a verified GraphBlueprint v2 hash. A one-parent object may bind one WorkUnit through the existing trailer identity. A two-parent object is a transport-only JoinBinding with no WorkUnit trailer and binds its two ordered parents, exactly one merge base, merge algorithm revision, reproducibly reconstructed clean candidate tree, observed merge tree, both parent-to-join upstream GraphReFly delta digests and join Blueprint hash. Missing or extra objects, cycles, non-ancestor base, width or range overflow, duplicate identity, zero or multiple merge bases, octopus parentage, merge WorkUnit trailer, candidate or Blueprint failure, tree mismatch, tamper and revision movement are fixed fail-closed errors. Git topology discovery and join reconstruction belong to Stack; GraphReFly remains responsible only for intrinsic Blueprint parsing, hash verification, rendering and pairwise structural delta facts.

The additive graphrefly.stack.semantic-dependency-graph.v2, work-unit-binding.v2, semantic-record.v2, dag-gate-input.v2 and dag-gate-result.v2 contracts keep semantic dependency edges distinct from C49 Git parent edges. WorkUnit identity remains stable and plan-scoped; every current WorkUnit resolves to exactly one reachable one-parent implementation binding, every declared dependency resolves to a reachable ancestor binding, dependency cycles or missing and duplicate bindings are errors, and unrelated Git ancestry creates no semantic dependency. SemanticChangeRecord v2 binds the canonically sorted direct dependency record IDs, exact current binding, Blueprint, claims, policy, source scope and checks. Evaluation validates the accepted plan as a topological order, uses WorkUnit ID as the independent-unit tie-break, orders joins by topological layer then Git OID, and preserves validation-stage, reason-code and canonical witness ordering. Gate pass requires every WorkUnit and join to be valid; invalidation recomputes only changed bindings, direct witness consumers, semantic dependants, Git joins and their affected descendants, while exact unaffected branch records may rebind immutably. Rebase or cherry-pick always creates a new binding and may use stable patch ID only as a candidate with rebindFrom evidence; two reachable bindings for one WorkUnit without explicit replacement lineage are ambiguous. Recovery through rebase, implementation change or selective replan produces new topology, input and result digests and never mutates old evidence or Git.

One repository-owned DAG domain operation supplies CLI, local review, pull-request integration and bounded GitHub merge_group adapters without reimplementing verdict semantics. The v2 review projection shows DagGateResult first; a bounded Git lane graph next; Git parent and optional semantic dependency edges with distinct encodings; ordinary commit intent, Blueprint and code evidence for its sole parent; and for a join, selectable evidence against each parent plus exact combined witnesses. Fully valid linear branch segments may collapse, but invalid objects, joins and dependency edges remain visible. The merge_group adapter accepts only an exact synthetic head whose full C49 slice and event identities validate, recomputes evidence on every attempt and cannot manage a queue, merge or mutate repositories. Existing v1 schemas, bytes, results and portable bundles remain verifiable unchanged. An explicit deterministic conversion maps a valid linear v1 plan, bindings and records into the single-parent subset of v2, produces new content identities and provenance, never edits the source artifacts, and rejects any v1 evidence that does not independently validate before conversion.

The strict graphrefly.stack.dag-review-evidence.v2 companion embeds one independently valid dag-gate-bundle.v2 or dag-structural-error-bundle.v2, its exact accepted plan and policy, verified public GraphBlueprint v2 evidence for every selected Git object, one canonical parent comparison for every topology edge with the upstream GraphReFly delta and server-parsed structured Git diff, and one derived dag-review.v2 navigation projection. Every nested source and digest is cross-bound and independently revalidated; missing, extra, reordered, forged or tampered objects, comparisons, hashes, selections, lanes, Git edges, semantic edges, verdicts or witnesses fail closed. Projection lanes preserve valid, invalid, not-evaluated and transport-only not-applicable states; default selection follows D40 and join parent switching changes only the selected comparison. The separate graphrefly.stack.dag-review-decision.v2 binds the complete GateResult, topology and dependency-graph digests, approve or request-changes, unverified local reviewer, optional selected-evidence context, summary and timestamp. It is append-only below Git common state, becomes stale after any bound digest changes, and never edits GateResult, approves only one branch, grants merge authority, executes repository code or enters portable or hosted evidence without explicit content-addressed export and policy redaction.

The canonical product exposes no linear-versus-DAG mode flag. grfs review independently resolves an exact ancestor base and head and inspects only the selected Git object topology: no plan plus a merge-free range selects structural linear review; an accepted plan plus a merge-free range selects the frozen semantic v1 runner; any selected merge commit requires an accepted plan and selects the v2 DAG domain operation. Source and Blueprint graph size never selects the runner. DAG repository identity comes from a parseable origin or one complete explicit provider, owner and name tuple; ambiguity, missing plan, partial identity, revision failure or ancestry drift fails closed before repository execution. review --dag is rejected rather than ignored. The single CI workflow generator has no --dag variant; pull_request and merge_group event adapters select the compatible frozen v1 or additive v2 operation only from independently validated event identity and Git topology while preserving old artifact verification and explicit conversion.
